notes: Impersonation: John Mitchell (operator) → David Kim (customer ec3031ac-6dd9-42e9-b7a7-0c10d9aac07b). Confirmed active at 1280px before resize.

rect.note: org label, no explicit rect measured

hamburgerItems.label: John Mitchell / logged in as / David Kim

rect.note: impersonation context label above the Back button

q1_direct_stop_entry.evidence: button[data-test-id='mobile-back-to-operator-button'] label='Back to John Mitchell' is present and visible in the hamburger nav panel. rect: 181×24px at top:489. This is a mobile-specific selector distinct from the desktop 'back-to-operator-row'. Clicking it successfully exited impersonation in 1 tap after the hamburger was already open.

q2_account_link_to_stop.yes_no: N/A — stop control is directly in hamburger, no /account detour needed
q2_account_link_to_stop.evidence: The hamburger panel itself renders 'John Mitchell / logged in as / David Kim' static text plus the 'Back to John Mitchell' button. There is no need to navigate to /account; the exit is surface-level in the hamburger panel.

q3_impersonation_cue_in_hamburger.evidence: Hamburger panel contains three visible impersonation cues: (1) StaticText 'John Mitchell' (operator), (2) StaticText 'logged in as', (3) StaticText 'David Kim' (customer). All rendered in the nav panel at 375px. Confirmed by a11y tree (uid=29_18, 29_19, 29_20) and textContent walker.

q4_stop_control_visible_on_home_mobile.yes_no: NO — not present in DOM at all when hamburger is closed
q4_stop_control_visible_on_home_mobile.evidence: At 375px on / with hamburger closed: querySelector('[data-test-id="back-to-operator-row"]') = null, querySelector('[data-test-id="mobile-back-to-operator-button"]') = null. Also operator-name-label, account-button, customer-name-label all null. Impersonation state is completely invisible on the page surface. Only accessible via hamburger open → panel contents.

q5_stop_control_visible_on_account_mobil.yes_no: NO — not present in DOM at all when hamburger is closed
q5_stop_control_visible_on_account_mobil.evidence: At 375px (≈500px actual) on /account/dashboard with hamburger closed: same result — all five impersonation selectors returned found:false. The page-level header does not render any impersonation UI on mobile regardless of route.

exitPaths.path: B: open hamburger → 'Back to John Mitchell' button directly visible in panel → tap
exitPaths.tapBreakdown: Tap 1: 'Main menu' hamburger button. Tap 2: 'Back to John Mitchell' button (data-test-id='mobile-back-to-operator-button').
exitPaths.endState: Operator session restored. Redirected to /company/members. Cart and notifications reverted to operator's own data (5 cart items, 1 notification vs David Kim's 10 / 2). No 'logged in as' in header.
exitPaths.note: Path A (hamburger → /account → exit) also technically works but requires 3 taps: hamburger open + Dashboard link + back button. Path B is shorter.

exitPaths.path: A: open hamburger → Dashboard link → exit via account page
exitPaths.succeeded: NOT_TESTED — unnecessary given Path B works directly

desktopControlComparison.note: Desktop renders 'back-to-operator-row' inside account-menu dropdown (opened via account-button). Mobile renders 'mobile-back-to-operator-button' directly in the hamburger nav panel — no nested dropdown required. Both are functional exit controls.

revisedFindingAssessment: F-02 'TRAP' framing requires downgrade. A functional mobile exit path exists via the hamburger panel in exactly 2 taps. The original finding correctly identified that the desktop top-header banner (operator-name-label + account-button) is not rendered at mobile viewport — this is accurate. However the hamburger panel IS a valid alternative mount point that surfaces: impersonation context labels, a direct 'Back to John Mitchell' button (mobile-back-to-operator-button), and a Logout button. The finding should be updated to: 'Mobile impersonation exit requires 2 extra taps (hamburger open + button) vs desktop 1-tap (account-menu dropdown already in header). Impersonation state is invisible until hamburger is opened — no persistent passive indicator exists on the mobile page surface. Severity: Low-Medium UX friction, not a trap.'
